does: This paper investigates label-flipping poisoning attacks against LLMs during RLHF and DPO alignment training. The authors formulate the minimum-cost poisoning problem as a convex optimization problem with linear constraints, deriving theoretical lower and upper bounds on the number of preference labels that must be flipped to steer an LLM toward an attacker's target policy. As a practical contribution, they propose a post-processing method that can reduce the number of required label flips for any existing poisoning attack while maintaining its effectiveness. Empirical validation demonstrates that this cost-minimization approach substantially decreases poisoning costs compared to baselines, with the greatest improvements when reward model dimensionality is small relative to dataset size.
connects: This work builds on recent empirical studies of data poisoning vulnerabilities in RLHF and DPO alignment procedures, extending prior research by establishing theoretical foundations that were previously absent. It connects to broader literature on adversarial attacks against machine learning systems and data poisoning in supervised learning, adapting these concepts to the reinforcement learning from human feedback setting. The paper responds to growing concerns about LLM robustness and security as these models become widely deployed, and relates to recent work examining vulnerabilities in preference learning and reward model manipulation.
enables: This theoretical framework opens avenues for developing defenses against label-flipping attacks by understanding their fundamental limits and structure. The cost-minimization perspective could guide future work on detecting poisoning by identifying implausibly low-cost label manipulations. The bounds derived here may inform certification and robustness verification methods for aligned LLMs. More broadly, the theoretical tools developed could extend to analyzing poisoning attacks in other preference-learning contexts beyond LLMs, including recommendation systems and other human-feedback-based training paradigms. The work also motivates investigation of more sophisticated poisoning strategies and hybrid attacks that combine label flipping with output modification.
